commentary: My latest letter ran in the Capital Gazette this morning. After 35 years watching land-use policy up close, I still believe the strongest conservation tool we have is a clear, well-enforced management plan. Grateful the editors gave it room to breathe.

A reminder as budget season approaches: public-lands stewardship is not a line item you can defer without cost. Deferred maintenance on federal land compounds like any other debt. We paid for that lesson once already in the 1990s.

Spoke to a full room at Bayview Community College this week on the history of environmental regulation. The students' questions were sharper than most I fielded in Washington. Encouraging to see young people take the long view on the Chesapeake watershed.

Reading a fine new history of the Bureau of Land Management's early decades. It is easy to forget how much of today's policy rests on decisions made by career staff whose names never appear in the headlines. Institutional memory matters.

On the anniversary of the Clean Water Act, a thought from retirement: regulation is rarely glamorous and almost never popular in the moment, but the Severn River is measurably healthier than the one I first fished in the 1980s. Progress is slow and worth defending.

My letter on shoreline erosion policy appeared in this week's Capital Gazette. To the reader who wrote in to disagree: you make a fair point on cost-sharing, and I would welcome the conversation over coffee. Good policy improves when it is argued honestly.

Honored to guest-lecture again at Bayview on land-management policy. Thirty-five years at Interior taught me that the hardest part of environmental policy is rarely the science; it is balancing competing legitimate interests over decades, not quarters.

A short piece I wrote on the value of public libraries ran in the Gazette. In retirement I tutor GED students at the Annapolis Library, and I am reminded weekly that access to a quiet room and a patient teacher is its own kind of public land.
